speaker: interviewer
text: Tape six. March 21, 1996. The interview is with Walter Rockler in DC, and I'm Bruce Einhorn. Mr. Rockler, just quickly, how many children do have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Four.

speaker: interviewer
text: From you second marriage? Four did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elliot, James, Nikolas, and Julia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did any of them follow you into the law business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Reluctantly, one did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest son worked as an economist for years and went to law school in his middle 30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elliot. But he worked as an economist for Indian tribes on Indian reservations, primarily the northern Cheyenne in Montana. And I think he finally reached the conclusion that it was better to be a lawyer, let's say, working for the Indians than to be an economist. So he went to law school out west somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did he have any contact with Nazi war criminals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. After he'd been in practice for a couple of years on Indian reservations, he went to work for OSI.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what OSI is.

speaker: interviewee
text: OSI is the Office of Special Investigations, which is a-- a very specialized office in the Department of Justice, US Department of Justice, whose activities consist primarily of investigating Nazis or Nazi accomplices or auxiliaries who entered the United States under false premises, lying and cheating their way through the-- through the barriers to entry, and in many cases, becoming naturalized. These were people who-- mainly Eastern Europeans, although I think some Germans and Austrians among them-- whom the office investigated with a view to possible deportation and denaturalization, primarily on the ground of fraud.

speaker: interviewer
text: You seem to know a lot about OSI.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do because I was the first director of it and set the office up back in 1979.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: By accident. How did it happen? A young partner of mine, with whom I was in good terms, went to work in the Department of Justice.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Arnold and Porter?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Arnold and Porter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this gentleman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Irv Nathan went to work for Phil Heymann, who was the head of the Criminal Division in the Department of Justice. One day, Irv called me up and said, we're thinking of setting something up in the department along the lines of OSI.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said this was in '79?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably the middle of '79. He said, can you give us any help in finding somebody who might be appropriate to run it? And I said, I'll ferret around and see. I'll call around and check with people. And I did. I called up Telford Taylor, asked him for suggestions. He didn't come up with much. I called up Ben Ferencz, talked to Drex Sprecher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is Drexel Sprecher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Drexel Sprecher was Taylor's deputy and so was Ben Ferencz.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: These were people who were relatively high-ranking officials when I was low on the totem pole at Nuremberg. Ben Ferencz was actually a classmate of mine in Harvard Law School, but he had served in this capacity after Nuremberg. He was the head of the Jewish restitution agency in Germany for many, many years, so he had an ongoing career founded on some of his Nuremberg experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did any of these alumni from Nuremberg give you a suggestion for the new head of OSI?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody came up with a good suggestion, so after about a few days of rummaging around, I called Irv back and said I haven't been able to locate anybody. Sorry. And he said, well, why don't you take it? I said, don't be crazy. You know, I'm 30 years removed from this stuff. I don't remember anything. I'm not into it. I talked to my wife. She didn't like the idea and her instincts weren't so crazy. And I said, don't be nuts. You know, I'm in the middle of a tax practice. It's true, I'm getting old, but I'm still into it. I was then about 59, 60 years old. And he said, well, come and talk to Heymann and we'll see if we-- we can work out something that's compatible with your taste. I said, I don't see how that's going to work, but I'll talk to Heymann and see. Maybe I can be helpful in some way. Well, I talked to Heymann. They made it really quite attractive. The basic understanding was I'd function for about six months. I would be responsible basically for setting the office up, hiring people, getting premises, and getting it started. No more than six months, on a contract basis, I would not be a regular employee of the Department of Justice. I had cases involving the Department of Justice on the other side, tax cases. We drafted a contract, which exempted those activities, so I could work for the Department of Justice and be suing it at the same time, and I did. And I was paid on an hourly basis at some midling-- I didn't give a damn what I was compensated at because my basic deal with the firm initially was whatever I got paid from the Department of Justice would be turned over to the firm and I would draw my normal partner's draw. The contract provided that I'd be an ongoing partner of Arnold and Porter. I wasn't going to quit Arnold and Porter because I knew they'd never take me back. [LAUGHS] Anyhow, that's the way it worked. In fact, after awhile I spent so damn much time at the Office of Special Investigation that I revised my agreement with the firm against interest and treated it as basically a sabbatical, which cut my firm take down by half. I thought I was cheating the firm. I had a designated deputy [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] who was the heir apparent to begin with. After six months, he would take over and run the place and I hired some of the people. He probably hired more. And we had a fairly good staff. First thing we lacked was a place to work. We didn't have an office anywhere. We didn't have a library, we didn't have secretaries, and I didn't understand government finance. We had a pretty good budget. I didn't have the slightest idea of how it worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't understand the main function of my job, which was public relations. I didn't like public relations. I didn't-- I would have two groups of people outside the government descending on me, Jewish groups who said how come you haven't hung anybody lately, or hanged them? You know, where have you've been? Who are you? And then I'd get the groups like Baltics, Ukrainians who said how come you're persecuting our-- our co-nationals?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't stand really either group. I had more sympathy to the Jewish groups, but they were haranguing me to produce some quick executions, and I knew I wasn't going to wind up producing some quick executions. And I thought that in various ways my designated deputy was attempting to undermine me while I was trying to carry on this goddamn job and I became quite incensed about that. And it all became very troublesome because the unnamed deputy was busy cultivating congressional figures that were important to this program, like Liz Holtzman, who was a supporter of his. He was busy dealing with somebody like Simon Wiesenthal, with whom he was close. And I would talk to him occasionally and said, you know, I'd like to meet with Wiesenthal and so forth. He never intimated that he knew anything about Wiesenthal. So I finally dealt with Wiesenthal directly and formed my own opinion of his operations.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all and all-- and then through-- through this deputy, there began to be a series of news releases about my-- my omissions and misconduct in office, which were just outrageous, since the office in terms of aggrandizement only cost me about $60,000 a year. All of this infuriated me and infuriated my wife more because she'd read these articles, which were planted, and get highly incensed about it. And I-- I had started with no animus with respect to this fellow at all. I took him around the firm. I took him out to some of the drinking sessions we had and I introduced him around. I figured, hell, he was going to take the damn thing over, what was his problem?

speaker: interviewee
text: I decided there was one thing I was going to do when I was in office and that was bring cases. So towards the end of my stay there, after about five, six months, we quickly ginned up six cases, which we threw into court and at least I had some kind of record left when I departed there, but I was actually there for about seven or eight months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you replace the deputy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sure did. Did I replace him? It was almost impossible to replace him. He was shunted off to another role in the Department of Justice. Meanwhile, I-- there was a fellow who worked in the solicitor's office with extremely good legal credentials--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Justice Department?

speaker: interviewee
text: Justice Department, Solicitor's office, in a very high level office of the Department of Justice. This was a fellow who had great academic credentials, had spent some time working for the, what I regard as, distinguished firm of Williams and Connolly and was in the solicitor's office. And he was interested in the job. I talked to him a couple times and initially told him I saw no prospects for him because my designated deputy was gonna get the job. That's all there was to it, and I never thought differently, but towards the end of the time I reached the conclusion that maybe it wasn't gonna work that way if I could help it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was this assistant to the solicitor general that you brought in?

speaker: interviewee
text: A fellow named Allan Ryan who became, ultimately, the next director, mainly because he looked good to me and I presented him, really, to Phil Heymann who probably was instrumental in making the decision and a fellow named Mark Richards who was also in the division. And that's the way it was resolved. But in many ways, it was not a gratifying experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you return to Arnold and Porter after OSI?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never left. As a matter of fact, it was a very hectic period for me because although I was about 60 years old at that point, I'd gotten back to a schedule which I didn't like, which is about 60 to 70 hours a week. I would spend two, three hours a day at Arnold and Porter, either early in the morning or late at night. And during the time, I remember-- and then I would spend the rest of the time at OSI. I was probably spending, oh, 2/3 of the time at OSI, which was why I renegotiated my deal with the firm. But I remember at one point working regularly at OSI, and at night, I would fly to New York and negotiate a contract for the sale of a business of one of our major clients, a major insurance company.

speaker: interviewer
text: For Arnold and Porter?

speaker: interviewee
text: For Arnold and Porter. I had this dual role. I-- I would have two sets of secretaries. I had one secretary, at least part time, at OSI. Then I had my regular secretary at my office at Arnold and Porter. I was taking taxicabs back and forth, every day of the week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Rockler, as we close, let me ask you, looking back on your services at Nuremberg, what do you think of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I never had any doubts as to the virtues of the Nuremberg Trials. I think, unfortunately, that they are still significant, but more symbolically significant than as controlling rules of conduct. The-- the cases established several principles, one of which was that you could set up an international court to try violations of international criminal law. A second was the defense that only states and not individuals are the subject of international law failed at Nuremberg. If it did nothing else, it established an irrefutable historical record of German conduct for a period of about 12 years, which stands there still as a monument to people who have now gotten around to say nothing ever happened. And the defense of superior orders was knocked down and reduced to a mitigating factor, and the charges, I thought, were interesting. Although there was a lot of dispute about them, the-- the basic Nuremberg charge was crimes against the peace, aggressive war. That was the real thrust of Nuremberg. It's still stands as a-- a testimonial to the principal that you cannot freely invade your neighbors at discretion, that-- that is a war crime, and of course, it's a war crime, which has been violated by every country in the succeeding 50 years. Unfortunately, it remains a war crime and something of a dead letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nuremberg also developed the concept of crimes against humanity, which is of considerable interest to a lot of people these days. And what is a crime against humanity? It's a crime that offends the conscience of mankind and really consists of a massive, atrocious war crime, since everything that's charged against crimes against humanity would also be barred by the Hague Conventions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, in practical effect, the Nuremberg Trials have not had a great deal of impact, although after a rocky start with people attacking one aspect or another, they've come to be accepted as-- as established international law, adhered to, in-- in theory, by all the countries of the world. They don't seem to have affected conduct at all. There has not been a major war on the scale of World War I or World War II, but to the extent there have been wars, there have been wars with very little justification and marked by very little civilized conduct. Of course, it may be a contradiction in terms to talk about a civilized war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, why do you think that the international community, while accepting the theoretical jurisdiction of the Nuremberg judgments, has ignored the rulings of Nuremberg in every day practice among states?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the so-called international community is a state of anarchy in which each state is an independent power, and if you've got industrial and military power, you do exactly as you please unless you face a counterveiling power. That was the status for a long time in the Cold War. The Soviets had power and did what they pleased. We had power and we did as we pleased.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now the Soviets have gone down the drain. There's one power left which thinks it can police the world and guess what that is. When a so-called national interest of any power is affected and international law would bar the exercise of that national interest, what do you think prevails, the national interest or international law?

speaker: interviewee
text: In other words, until such time as you have the elements of an international community with some of the apparatus of a-- of a bona fide institutional international community, you are not going to have any application of international law on a systematic basis. You will have an application when a dominant power says now we want to enforce it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think that's going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think some of it has happened now, but this is the primitive stage, where a dominant power says now we'll enforce it for other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your grandchildren think about Nuremberg, what do you want them to think about and about your role there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think in the current context of-- of world affairs, I'm not so sure my grandchildren will grow up and do a lot of thinking altogether. Two-- two principles were not covered at Nuremberg, one is indiscriminate air bombing is not a crime. Technically it's a crime, but it's been engaged in endlessly. In other words, German bombing and Allied bombing were not declared war crimes and those are two of the deadliest forms of warfare around. Second, there is no bar to the use of nuclear weapons and there are more countries that have nuclear weapons. Now, if you feel comfortable with the notion that this balance will always be maintained-- I do not because I think somewhere someone's going to slip up and a lot of nuclear bombs will go off. And I don't see that as a great future for mankind. In other words, I think the existing state of human affairs is dismal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lastly, what did the Nuremberg experience and the OSI experience do to you as a-- as a Jew? Did it have any effect on your Jewishness, on your consciousness as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was conscious that I was a Jew before, and I was conscious that I was a Jew afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Rockler, thank you very much.
